subject: maths
title: Surface Areas and Volumes

nodes.title: Find surface areas and volumes of combinations of solids
nodes.brief: Real objects are built from two or more basic solids; to find their surface area or volume you must decompose the object and recombine the parts correctly.
nodes.quote: In this chapter, you will see how to find surface areas and volumes of such objects.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Recognise the object as a combination of basic solids.
Real objects such as a toy top, a rocket, a bird-bath, or a silo are not pure spheres or cylinders — they are built from two or more of the basic solids (cuboid, cone, cylinder, sphere, hemisphere). The first step is always to name each constituent solid and identify where it is joined to its neighbour.

PART 2 — Decide whether surface area or volume is wanted, then apply the correct rule.
For surface area, add only the exposed curved surfaces (joined flat faces vanish). For volume, simply add all the parts' volumes (joining does not remove interior space). Choosing the wrong rule — adding total surface areas, or subtracting a volume that does not disappear — is the most common error.

PART 3 — Decompose, compute each part, and combine.
Once you know which formula to apply, compute each part separately using the relevant bedrock formula, then add (or subtract, for a cavity) the results. For example, a toy = hemisphere + cone: volume = (2/3)πr³ + (1/3)πr²h.
keyMoves: Recognise the object as a combination of two basic solids
Decide whether surface area or volume is wanted
Decompose, compute each part, and combine correctly
misconceptions: Treating a combined solid as a single basic solid
Adding total surface areas (instead of curved) when joining

nodes.title: Decompose a combined solid into basic solids
nodes.brief: Before applying any formula, name each constituent solid in the object and note where it is attached.
nodes.quote: We can see that this solid is made up of a cylinder with two hemispheres stuck at either end.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Compare the object's shape to the four basic solids.
The four basic solids used throughout this chapter are: cuboid (box), cylinder (can), cone (ice-cream cone shape), and hemisphere (half-ball). Look at each distinct region of the combined object and decide which basic solid it matches — by its flat base, curved side, or rounded top.

PART 2 — Name each constituent and where it is attached.
State exactly what is joined to what: for example, "a cylinder with a hemisphere stuck at each end" or "a cone placed on top of a hemisphere of the same radius." This two-part description drives every later formula choice. A depression (cavity) counts as a removed solid, not an added one.
keyMoves: Compare the object's parts to the four basic solids
Name each constituent and where it is attached
misconceptions: Misnaming a hemisphere as a full sphere
Failing to notice a depression/cavity counts as a removed solid

nodes.title: Total surface area of a combination = sum of curved surface areas
nodes.brief: When two solids are joined, the hidden flat faces vanish; total surface area = sum of exposed curved surfaces only.
nodes.quote: the total surface area of the new solid is the sum of the curved surface areas of each of the individual parts
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — List the exposed curved surfaces; joined flat faces vanish.
When two solids are glued together, each flat face at the join is invisible (hidden inside). The visible outer surface consists only of the curved portions of each part. Never add the full TSA of each part — that double-counts the hidden faces.

PART 2 — Sum their CSAs, adjusting for special cases (covered patches, exposed rings).
Most assemblies: TSA = CSA1 + CSA2. But if one solid's flat base is larger (ring exposed) or if a flat patch of a cube is covered, those areas are added or subtracted as needed. The general rule is: total visible area = sum of all curved surfaces that remain exposed after joining.
misconceptions: Adding the full TSA of each part
Forgetting that the join hides two flat faces

nodes.title: TSA of a cone surmounted by a hemisphere
nodes.brief: A toy top (cone + hemisphere of equal radius) has total surface area = CSA hemisphere + CSA cone.
nodes.quote: Total surface area of the toy = CSA of hemisphere + CSA of cone
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The two curved surfaces share the same radius; the flat join circle is hidden.
When a cone is placed on a hemisphere so that the cone's base fits exactly on the hemisphere's flat face, the shared circle is invisible. The entire outer surface is just the dome (2πr²) plus the slanted cone (πrl). Both use the same radius r.

PART 2 — Add CSA of hemisphere (2pir^2) and CSA of cone (pirl).
Example 1 (lattu): r = 1.75 cm, l ≈ 3.7 cm. CSA hemisphere = 2π(1.75²) = 2π(3.0625) ≈ 19.25 cm². CSA cone = π(1.75)(3.7) ≈ 20.35 cm². Total TSA ≈ 19.25 + 20.35 = 39.6 cm².
keyMoves: Set hemisphere radius = cone base radius
Add CSA of hemisphere (2pir^2) and CSA of cone (pirl)
misconceptions: Including the flat join circle
Using sphere CSA 4πr² instead of hemisphere 2πr²

nodes.title: Curved surface area of a hemisphere = 2πr²
nodes.brief: The curved (dome) surface of a hemisphere is half the full sphere's surface: CSA = 2πr².
nodes.quote: the curved surface area of the hemisphere = 1/2 (4πr²) = 2πr²
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Start from the full sphere's surface area, then halve it.
A complete sphere of radius r has surface area 4πr² (a standard Class IX result). A hemisphere is exactly half a sphere, so its curved (dome) surface is (1/2)(4πr²) = 2πr². This is the only curved surface visible on the outside; the flat circular base (πr²) is a separate piece added only when a solid hemisphere is needed.

PART 2 — Always use the radius, not the diameter.
If the problem gives a diameter d, halve it first: r = d/2. Then substitute into 2πr². For example, a hemisphere of diameter 4 cm has r = 2 cm, so CSA = 2π(4) = 8π ≈ 25.13 cm².
misconceptions: Using 4πr² (full sphere)
Including the flat circular base

nodes.title: Curved surface area of a cylinder = 2πrh
nodes.brief: The side (lateral) surface of a cylinder wraps around once: CSA = 2πrh.
nodes.quote: = 2πrh + 2πr² = 2πr (h + r)
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The curved surface is circumference times height.
If you unroll the side wall of a cylinder of radius r and height h, you get a rectangle of width 2πr (the circumference) and height h, so CSA = 2πrh. Do not include the two circular ends unless the problem explicitly asks for the full TSA (= 2πrh + 2πr²).

PART 2 — Add base areas only when the problem requires them.
For a combined solid, the exposed bases are usually covered by another part or not visible, so CSA alone is used. For example, the bird-bath cylinder (r = 30 cm, h = 145 cm) contributes CSA = 2π(30)(145) = 8700π cm² ≈ 27342 cm².
keyMoves: Add base areas only when the problem requires them
misconceptions: Confusing CSA with TSA (which adds 2πr²)
Using diameter instead of radius

nodes.title: Curved surface area of a cone = pirl
nodes.brief: The slanted surface of a cone uses slant height l, not vertical height h: CSA = pirl.
nodes.quote: Therefore, CSA of cone = πrl = ( 22/7 × 3.5/2 × 3.7 ) cm²
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Use the slant height l, not the vertical height h.
The curved surface of a cone is the slanted outer skin, which depends on how long the slant is. The formula is CSA = pirl, where l is the slant height (the distance along the slope from base rim to apex). Using h instead of l is the most common error and always gives a wrong answer.

PART 2 — Compute pirl with l already found.
For the lattu in Example 1: r = 1.75 cm, l ≈ 3.7 cm. CSA = (22/7)(1.75)(3.7) = (22/7)(6.475) ≈ 20.35 cm². Slant height must be computed first (see compute-slant-height node) unless it is given directly.
keyMoves: Use slant height l (not vertical height h)
misconceptions: Using vertical height h in place of slant height l
Including the base circle in CSA

nodes.title: Compute slant height l = sqrt(r^2 + h^2)
nodes.brief: A cone's slant height l is the hypotenuse of the right triangle formed by its radius r and vertical height h.
nodes.quote: the slant height of the cone (l) = √(r² + h²) = √( (3.5/2)² + (3.25)² ) cm = 3.7 cm (approx.)
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The slant height is the hypotenuse of a right triangle.
A cross-section of a cone reveals a right triangle: vertical height h (one leg), base radius r (other leg), and the slant edge l (hypotenuse). By the Pythagoras theorem (covered in Chapter 7): l = sqrt(r² + h²). The slant height l — not the vertical height h — goes into the CSA formula πrl.

PART 2 — Square r and h separately, add, then take the square root.
For the lattu in Example 1: r = 3.5/2 = 1.75 cm, h = 3.25 cm. Then l = sqrt(1.75² + 3.25²) = sqrt(3.0625 + 10.5625) = sqrt(13.625) ≈ 3.7 cm. Always compute r² and h² individually before adding.
keyMoves: Square r and h, add them
misconceptions: Adding r + h instead of sqrt(r²+h²)
Swapping slant height and vertical height

nodes.title: Derive a part's height by subtracting from the total height
nodes.brief: When only the total height of a combined solid is given, back out each part's height by subtraction.
nodes.quote: the height of the cone = height of the top – height (radius) of the hemispherical part
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify what the total height spans.
Problems usually give the overall height of the combined object, not the height of each part separately. Total height = height of part 1 + contribution of part 2. For a cone sitting on a hemisphere, the hemisphere's contribution to the total height equals its radius r.

PART 2 — Subtract the known part's contribution to get the unknown part's height.
Example 1 (lattu): total height = 5 cm, hemisphere radius = 3.5/2 = 1.75 cm, so cone height h = 5 − 1.75 = 3.25 cm. In Example 3 (rocket), cylinder height h' = total − cone height. Always subtract the radius (not the diameter) of the hemispherical part.
keyMoves: Subtract the known part's contribution to get the unknown part's height
misconceptions: Using the total height as a single part's height
Subtracting diameter when radius should be subtracted

nodes.title: TSA of a cube with a hemisphere attached on a face
nodes.brief: Attaching a hemisphere on a cube's face hides a circle but exposes a dome: TSA = 6a^2 - pir^2 + 2pir^2.
nodes.quote: So, the surface area of the block = TSA of cube – base area of hemisphere + CSA of hemisphere
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Start with the cube's full surface area, then correct for the covered patch.
A cube of edge a has TSA = 6a². When a hemisphere of radius r is glued to one face, the flat base of the hemisphere (a circle of area πr²) covers part of that face and becomes invisible. Subtract πr².

PART 2 — Add the dome's curved surface.
The dome (CSA = 2πr²) is now the visible part of the hemisphere. So surface area of block = 6a² − πr² + 2πr² = 6a² + πr². Example 2: a = 5 cm, r = 2.1 cm. Area = 6(25) + π(4.41) = 150 + 13.86 = 163.86 cm².
keyMoves: Compute TSA of cube 6 times edge squared
misconceptions: Adding the full hemisphere TSA without removing the covered base
Subtracting a whole cube face instead of the circle

refreshers.trigger: Student does not recall that a cube of edge a has total surface area 6a^2 (six congruent square faces, each of area a^2) - the Class IX result this node's derivation starts from and never re-derives.
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: quick one before we go on: where does the 6a^2 for the cube come from - why six, and why a^2?
ladder: A cube is a box whose edges are all the same length a (like a dice or a sugar cube).
How many flat faces does a cube have? Count them: top and bottom, front and back, left and right - that is 6 faces.
Each face is a square with side a. What is the area of one such square? (Area of a square = side times side.)
If one face has area a times a = a^2, and there are 6 identical faces, what is the area of all of them added together?
So the whole outer surface of the cube is 6 lots of a^2, which is 6 times a^2 = 6a^2.
refreshers.answer: A cube has six faces, and because all its edges are equal each face is an identical square of side a. The area of one square face is side times side = a times a = a^2, and since the six faces are exactly the same, the total surface area is 6 of them added up: 6 times a^2 = 6a^2. This is a standard Class IX result, measured in square units (cm^2, m^2), and it is taken as known here, not re-derived. In this node that 6a^2 is the starting figure: we begin with the cube's full surface 6a^2, then subtract the small circle (pi*r^2) hidden under the hemisphere's flat base and add the dome (2*pi*r^2) on top, giving 6a^2 + pi*r^2.
refreshers.returnCue: with the cube's 6a^2 secure, let us correct it for the hemisphere: take 6a^2 away the covered circle, add the dome.

nodes.title: TSA when a hemispherical depression is cut into a solid
nodes.brief: A hemispherical cavity scooped into a cylinder's top adds the dome's inner curved surface to the cylinder's side.
nodes.quote: the total surface area of the bird-bath = CSA of cylinder + CSA of hemisphere
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — A cavity exposes curved area, not solid volume.
When a hemispherical depression (hollow) is carved into the flat top of a cylinder, the flat top is replaced by the inner curved surface of the depression. The visible surface is now: the cylinder's side wall (CSA = 2πrh) plus the curved inside of the hemispherical bowl (CSA = 2πr²). There is no flat top left.

PART 2 — Add CSA of cylinder + CSA of hemisphere.
Example 4 (bird-bath): r = 30 cm (= 45/2 dm, but given directly as 30 cm), h = 145 cm. Total surface area = 2π(30)(145) + 2π(30²) = 8700π + 1800π = 10500π cm² = 33000 cm² = 3.3 m².
keyMoves: Treat the depression's inner dome as exposed curved surface
Add CSA of cylinder + CSA of hemisphere
misconceptions: Counting the depression as added solid
Adding a flat base area where the cavity opens

nodes.title: Account for an exposed base ring when stacked radii differ
nodes.brief: When the cone's base is wider than the cylinder it sits on, the exposed annular ring must also be painted.
nodes.quote: a part of the base of the cone (a ring) is to be painted
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Compare the two radii to spot the exposed ring.
In Example 3 (rocket), the cone has base radius r = 2.5 cm and the cylinder has radius r' = 1.5 cm. Because r > r', the cone's base does not fit exactly on the cylinder top; an annular ring (the donut-shaped area between the two circles) is left exposed and must be included in the painted surface.

PART 2 — Add the annular ring area = pir^2 - pi(r')^2.
Orange region (cone + ring): CSA of cone + ring area = πrl + (πr² − π(r')²) = π(2.5)(6.5) + π(2.5² − 1.5²) = 51.05 + π(4) ≈ 51.05 + 12.57 = 63.62 cm². (NCERT rounds to 63.585 cm².)
keyMoves: Add the annular ring area = pir^2 - pi(r')^2
misconceptions: Ignoring the exposed ring
Subtracting the wrong base

nodes.title: Painted area of a cylinder part = CSA + one base circle
nodes.brief: When one end of a cylinder is capped by another solid, the painted area is the side wall plus only the exposed bottom base.
nodes.quote: = 2πr'h' + π(r')²
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The top is covered; only the bottom base is exposed.
In Example 3 (rocket, yellow cylindrical region), the cylinder's top is covered by the cone sitting on it, so only the cylinder's curved wall and its single bottom base are painted. This is CSA + one base, not the full TSA (which would add two bases).

PART 2 — Add CSA = 2pir'h' and one base = pi(r')^2; factor as pir'(2h' + r').
With r' = 1.5 cm and h' = 20 cm: area = 2π(1.5)(20) + π(1.5²) = 60π + 2.25π = 62.25π ≈ 195.47 cm². (NCERT gives 195.465 cm² using π = 3.14.)
keyMoves: Add CSA of cylinder 2pir'h' to one base circle pi(r')^2
misconceptions: Adding both base circles (full TSA)
Forgetting the single exposed base entirely (pure CSA)

nodes.title: Total surface area of a solid hemisphere = 3pir^2
nodes.brief: A solid hemisphere has both the dome (2pir^2) and the flat circular base (pir^2), so TSA = 3pir^2.
nodes.quote: = 150 – πr² + 2πr² = (150 + πr²) cm²
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Distinguish CSA from TSA for a hemisphere.
The curved surface area (CSA) of a hemisphere is 2πr² — just the dome. But a solid hemisphere also has a flat circular base of area πr². If the question asks for the total surface area (TSA) of a standalone solid hemisphere, both surfaces count.

PART 2 — Add dome and base: TSA = 2pir^2 + pir^2 = 3pir^2.
For example, a solid hemisphere of radius 7 cm: TSA = 3π(49) = 147π ≈ 462 cm². This formula is directly tested in the CBSE SQP (Basic Q14). Do not confuse with the cube+hemisphere node where the flat base is already hidden by the cube's face.
misconceptions: Reporting only 2πr² (forgetting the base)
Using 4πr² (full sphere)

nodes.title: Volume of a combination = sum of the parts volumes
nodes.brief: Joining solids hides surface area but not interior space; total volume is simply the sum of each part's volume.
nodes.quote: The volume of the solid formed by joining two basic solids will actually be the sum of the volumes of the constituents
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Unlike surface area, joining does not remove volume.
When two solids are glued together, the interior space of each is preserved — no volume is lost at the join (the hidden faces are inside the material, not air). So V_total = V_part1 + V_part2. This contrast with the surface-area rule (which subtracts hidden faces) is the most important conceptual distinction in the chapter.

PART 2 — Compute each part's volume independently, then add.
Example 5 (shed): cuboid part = 840 m³, half-cylinder part ≈ 288.75 m³; total = 1128.75 m³. Example 7 (toy): hemisphere = (2/3)π(8) + cone = (1/3)π(4)(2) = 8π ≈ 25.12 cm³. A cavity (depression) is the one exception: subtract the cavity's volume.
misconceptions: Subtracting an overlap that does not exist
Carrying over the 'curved only' rule from surface area

nodes.title: Volume of a cylinder = pir^2h
nodes.brief: A cylinder's volume equals the area of its circular base times its height: V = pir^2h.
nodes.quote: the apparent capacity of the glass = πr²h
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Square the radius to get the base area, then scale by height.
The circular base of a cylinder has area πr². Multiplying by height h gives the volume: V = πr²h. This is the standard Class IX result used throughout Chapter 12 — for the cylindrical glass (Example 6), the full silo body, and the shaft in Example 5.

PART 2 — Always use radius, not diameter, and keep units consistent.
If the diameter is given, halve it first. Example 6: inner radius r = 2.5 cm (diameter 5 cm given), h = 10 cm. V = π(6.25)(10) = 62.5π = 196.25 cm³ (apparent capacity).
keyMoves: Multiply by pi and height h
misconceptions: Using diameter for r
Confusing with cone volume (missing the 1/3)

nodes.title: Volume of a cone = (1/3)pir^2h
nodes.brief: A cone holds exactly one-third of the cylinder with the same base and height: V = (1/3)pir^2h.
nodes.quote: So, volume of the toy = 2/3 πr³ + 1/3 πr²h
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Compute pir^2h first, then multiply by 1/3.
The volume of a cone is one-third of the cylinder with the same circular base (area πr²) and height h: V = (1/3)πr²h. The 1/3 factor is essential and is the most common source of errors when computing toy/rocket volumes.

PART 2 — Use vertical height h (not slant height l).
Example 7 (toy): cone has r = 2 cm, h = 2 cm. V = (1/3)π(4)(2) = 8π/3 ≈ 8.38 cm³. The slant height l is used only in the CSA formula; for volume always use the vertical height h.
misconceptions: Dropping the 1/3 factor
Using slant height in place of vertical height

nodes.title: Volume of a hemisphere = (2/3)pir^3
nodes.brief: A hemisphere holds half a sphere's volume: V = (2/3)pir^3.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Start from the sphere's volume, then halve it.
A full sphere of radius r has volume (4/3)πr³ (a standard Class IX result). A hemisphere is half a sphere, so its volume is (2/3)πr³. Note that the power is r³ (cube), not r² — using r² is a common slip.

PART 2 — Cube the radius, then multiply by (2/3)pi.
Example 6 (glass with hemispherical bump): r = 2.5 cm. V = (2/3)π(2.5³) = (2/3)π(15.625) = (2/3)(3.14)(15.625) ≈ 32.71 cm³. This hemisphere reduces the usable capacity of the glass.
misconceptions: Using (4/3)πr³ (full sphere)
Using r² instead of r³

nodes.title: Volume of a half cylinder = (1/2)pir^2h
nodes.brief: A half-cylinder (semicircular cross-section) has volume equal to half the full cylinder's volume.
nodes.quote: the required volume = volume of the cuboid + 1/2 volume of the cylinder
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify which dimension is the cylinder's length (not its height in the usual orientation).
In Example 5, the shed roof is a cylinder lying on its side: the cylinder's length runs along the 15 m direction, and its radius is half the 7 m span = 3.5 m. The 'height' of the cylinder formula is 15 m (the length of the shed).

PART 2 — Compute full cylinder volume, then halve it.
Full cylinder volume = πr²h = π(3.5²)(15) = π(12.25)(15) = 183.75π ≈ 577.5 m³. Half-cylinder volume = 577.5/2 = 288.75 m³. (Using π = 22/7: (22/7)(12.25)(15)/2 = 288.75 m³.)
misconceptions: Forgetting the 1/2
Mixing up the cylinder length with the cuboid height

nodes.title: Volume of a cuboid = l x b x h
nodes.brief: A cuboid (box) has volume equal to the product of its three dimensions: V = l x b x h.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Multiply all three dimensions: length, breadth (width), height.
A cuboid has a rectangular cross-section; its volume is V = l x b x h (standard Class IX). In Example 5 (shed), the cuboidal part has l = 15 m, b = 7 m, h = 8 m, giving V = 15 x 7 x 8 = 840 m³.

PART 2 — Use consistent units; avoid using only two dimensions.
A common error is computing l x b (area, not volume). Ensure all three dimensions are in the same unit, then multiply. The total shed volume in Example 5 is cuboid volume + half-cylinder volume = 840 + 288.75 = 1128.75 m³.
misconceptions: Using only two dimensions (area not volume)
Mismatching units

nodes.title: Actual capacity = apparent capacity minus volume of the raised part
nodes.brief: A hemispherical bump at the base of a glass occupies space; actual capacity = cylinder volume minus hemisphere volume.
nodes.quote: the actual capacity of the glass = apparent capacity of glass – volume of the hemisphere
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The bump displaces space; apparent capacity overstates reality.
Example 6: a cylindrical glass has a hemispherical bump on its inner base (the glass manufacturer does this to save material). The cylinder's volume πr²h is the apparent (theoretical) capacity, but the hemisphere occupies volume inside the glass, leaving less room for liquid.

PART 2 — Subtract the hemisphere's volume from the cylinder's volume.
With r = 2.5 cm, h = 10 cm: apparent = π(6.25)(10) = 196.25 cm³. Hemisphere volume = (2/3)π(15.625) ≈ 32.71 cm³. Actual capacity = 196.25 − 32.71 = 163.54 cm³ ≈ 163.54 mL.
misconceptions: Adding the hemisphere instead of subtracting
Forgetting the displacement entirely

nodes.title: Find volume difference between a circumscribing cylinder and the solid
nodes.brief: The empty space around a toy inside its just-enclosing cylinder = cylinder volume minus toy volume.
nodes.quote: the volume required = volume of the right circular cylinder – volume of the toy
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Build the circumscribing cylinder: same radius, height = sum of parts.
Example 7: the toy (hemisphere radius r = 2 cm, cone height h = 2 cm) fits inside a cylinder of radius 2 cm and height = hemisphere radius + cone height = 2 + 2 = 4 cm. This cylinder just encloses the toy with no extra room to the sides or top.

PART 2 — Subtract the toy's volume from the cylinder's volume.
Cylinder volume = πr²H = π(4)(4) = 16π ≈ 50.24 cm³. Toy volume = (2/3)πr³ + (1/3)πr²h = (2/3)π(8) + (1/3)π(4)(2) = 16π/3 + 8π/3 = 24π/3 = 8π ≈ 25.12 cm³. Empty space = 50.24 − 25.12 = 25.12 cm³.
keyMoves: Set cylinder height = sum of the parts' heights
misconceptions: Wrong cylinder height (forgetting to add both parts)
Subtracting in the wrong order

nodes.title: Keep units consistent before computing
nodes.brief: When a problem mixes cm and m, convert everything to one unit first; then convert the final answer if needed.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Convert all lengths to a common unit before substituting.
Example 4 (bird-bath) gives the height in metres (1.45 m) and radius in centimetres (30 cm). Using different units in a single formula produces a nonsense answer. Choose one unit — here, convert 1.45 m = 145 cm — and use it for all measurements in the calculation.

PART 2 — Convert the final answer to the requested unit.
Area conversions: 1 m = 100 cm, so 1 m² = 10 000 cm² (not 100 cm²). Volume: 1 m³ = 1 000 000 cm³. For the bird-bath: total area = 33 000 cm² = 33 000 / 10 000 m² = 3.3 m².
keyMoves: Convert all lengths to a common unit
Convert the final area/volume to requested units
misconceptions: Mixing cm and m in one product
Using 1 m² = 100 cm² instead of 10000

nodes.title: Use radius = diameter divided by 2
nodes.brief: Every area and volume formula in this chapter needs the radius; if a diameter is given, halve it first.
nodes.quote: The radius BO of the hemisphere (as well as of the cone) = 1/2 × 4 cm = 2 cm.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Halve the given diameter to get the radius.
Problems in this chapter commonly state the diameter (e.g. 'a hemisphere of diameter 4 cm'). All surface-area and volume formulas — 2πr², πr²h, (2/3)πr³, etc. — use the radius r, not the diameter. The fix is always r = diameter / 2 before substituting into any formula.

PART 2 — Use that radius consistently in all formulas for that solid.
Example 7 (toy): hemisphere and cone share the same base — diameter 4 cm, so r = 2 cm for both. Plugging diameter 4 into a formula that expects r would give answers 4 times too large (in area) or 8 times too large (in volume).
keyMoves: Use that radius in all formulas
misconceptions: Plugging diameter directly into formulas
Halving the radius again by mistake

narrative:
## What was read
I re-read the full segmented chapter `jemh112.txt` (SEG 0 Introduction → SEG 3 Summary; 7 worked Examples) and the shared CBSE SQP + Marking Scheme `_maths-exams.txt` (Standard Q5 cone=sphere, Standard Q38 metallic silo, Basic Q14 solid-hemisphere TSA). I located every node and gate quote verbatim in the segment it is tagged to before approving it.

## Corrections made to the draft
1. **Citation sec fix (D-120):** `radius-from-diameter` was tagged `sec:1`, but its quote "The radius BO of the hemisphere (as well as of the cone) = 1/2 × 4 cm = 2 cm." lives at line 217, which is inside **SEG 2** (12.3, Example 7), not SEG 1. Retagged to `sec:2`. All other 23 node quotes were confirmed character-for-character in their tagged SEG (e.g. `csa-cylinder` "= 2πrh + 2πr² = 2πr (h + r)" at line 136 SEG 1; `volume-combination-sum-of-volumes` rule at line 169 SEG 2).
2. **Grain — added one missed failable skill:** Example 3's *yellow* region is a distinct computation the draft omitted — painting a cylinder's curved wall PLUS exactly one (the open bottom) base: area = πr'(2h' + r') = 195.465 cm². This contrasts with both pure CSA and full TSA, so I added node `painted-area-cylinder-with-one-open-base` (intermediate, SEG 1, quote "= 2πr'h' + π(r')²" line 118), edged it under `tsa-combination-sum-of-csa` → `csa-cylinder`, and gave it an answer-stated gate (195.465, quote "= 4.71 × 41.5 cm²\n\n= 195.465 cm²", lines 124-126).

## Dependency tree
Single root `surface-areas-and-volumes-of-combinations` → two rule hubs (sum-of-CSA, sum-of-volumes) → specific assembly skills (toy, cube+hemisphere, depression, ring, yellow-cylinder, displacement, circumscribing-cylinder, solid-hemisphere TSA) → bedrock formulas → shared numeracy (`radius-from-diameter`, `convert-units-cm-to-m`). Verified acyclic, single-rooted, no orphan (the new node is edged both up and down), and every edge endpoint is a defined slug. 25 nodes, 25 gates (one per node, 100% coverage).

## Honest uncertainty / absent
Transcription garbles some fractions ("3.5/2" for radius 1.75; CSA-hemisphere shown as "1/2 (4πr²)"); I copied quotes exactly as garbled per D-120 rather than normalising. `volume-cuboid`'s gate quote is the combined shed line (1128.75) — the chapter never isolates the bare 15×7×8 = 840 sub-result on its own line, so the quote supports the prompt's computation but states the larger sum; the numeric grader still passes on 840. The pencil exam item (Basic Q38) duplicates the silo skill set, so I sourced the cleaner silo gates instead. No symbolic/CAS gates were used (numeric/MCQ only) to keep grading deterministic.
absentNotes: The chapter (2024-25 NCERT) does NOT teach: (1) frustum of a cone — slant-height/CSA/TSA/volume of a frustum were removed from this edition and appear nowhere in jemh112, so no node. (2) Conversion/recasting of solids (melting and recasting one solid into another) — not present in this chapter's worked Examples (only hinted at in unanswered Exercise 12.2 Q5/Q7). (3) Surface area/volume of a standalone sphere, cylinder, cone, or cuboid as DERIVATIONS — these are assumed from Class IX ("you have also learnt how to find their surface areas and volumes") and only reused here, so they are bedrock formula-application nodes, not derivation skills. (4) Any proof of πr²h, (4/3)πr³, 2πr² etc. — the chapter only applies them. (5) The Exercises (12.1, 12.2) state problems but the text gives no answers, so they were not used as gate sources; only answer-stating worked Examples and the SQP/Marking-Scheme were. (6) The Introduction (SEG 0) and Summary (SEG 3) are pure exposition/recap and contribute no failable skill except the goal-level scope statement (used for the goal node + its MCQ gate).
